Paul begins this chapter with a stunning claim: you have died. Not metaphorically—you have actually died to the law through the body of Christ. He illustrates this with a widow whose former marriage contract no longer binds her after her husband's death. She is free, not because the law was unjust, but because death has dissolved the relationship. This is your condition now. The law that once had authority over you has been rendered powerless not by being repealed but by your death in Christ and resurrection into new life with Him. You have been released from that bond so you might belong to another—to Christ Himself—and bear fruit to God. This is not the end of obligation; it is the end of bondage to a taskmaster and the beginning of love for a Bridegroom.

But then Paul turns inward, and what follows is perhaps the most honest description of spiritual struggle in all of Scripture. He doesn't offer quick resolution. Instead, he takes you into the war between what he knows to be good and what he finds himself actually doing. The law itself is holy and righteous—Paul affirms this repeatedly. But the law, though good, has exposed the depths of sin within him. It shows him coveting, rebellion, death itself. The commandment that promised life has revealed only death. And in verses 14-25, Paul describes his own experience of being "sold as a slave to sin"—wanting desperately to do good, delighting in God's law in his inner being, yet finding evil present with him at every turn. He does the very thing he hates. This is not the testimony of an unbeliever; it is the anguished cry of a regenerate person who knows the goodness of God's law but experiences the reality of remaining sin.

The chapter ends not with Paul finding peace within himself but with an urgent cry: "What a wretched man I am! Who will rescue me from this body of death?" And then, the answer: "Thanks be to God, through Jesus Christ our Lord!" Notice that Paul does not claim to have solved the inner conflict. He serves God's law with his mind but remains bound by the law of sin in his flesh. This is your reality too. The gospel does not promise that you will stop struggling with sin in this life. It promises that Christ Himself is your rescue, that you are no longer condemned, and that the struggle itself testifies to your new life in Him. You are not the person you were before, even though the old war still rages within you. Your rescue is secure, not because you have achieved victory, but because Christ has already won it and given it to you.
